draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond more to animated metrics that breathe with their stride than static readouts
facade.label: Pace pulses with data
facade.acceptImplies: Build live-updating metrics that animate in sync with GPS refresh, not static number blocks
facade.rejectImplies: Keep metrics stable and readable—animation risks distraction during effort-heavy runs

evidence.hypothesis: Runners respond more to animated metrics that breathe with their stride than static readouts
evidence.implication: Build live-updating metrics that animate in sync with GPS refresh, not static number blocks

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond to warm, luminous data visualization over cold or muted displays
facade.label: Glowing metrics
facade.acceptImplies: User values warmth and approachability in data readout; likely drawn to amber/orange accent colors and soft glow effects on performance numbers
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers cooler, more clinical visual language; may favor high-contrast, minimal, or monochromatic metric displays

facade.hypothesis: Runner-focused weather apps benefit from clean white backgrounds and high-contrast data to reduce cognitive load during pre-run scanning
facade.label: Bright minimalist
facade.acceptImplies: User values clarity and speed over ambient mood; prefers iOS-style simplicity; likely wants data-forward layout
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer darker interfaces, atmospheric design, or more visual richness; possibly values mood/immersion over pure legibility

facade.hypothesis: Runners prefer prominent metrics (pace, distance, time) over narrative explanations of conditions
facade.label: Big numbers, small context
facade.acceptImplies: Design around large, scannable data points. Bury weather explanation. Numbers are the story.
facade.rejectImplies: Runners want weather story told in prose or icons first. Numbers serve context, not lead it.

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize quick metric checks mid-run; bottom navigation keeps thumbs in safe zone and eyes low on screen
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure: sidebar vs. bottom tabs vs. top tabs
facade.label: Bottom tabs for stats
facade.acceptImplies: User values thumb-friendly navigation and frequent stat glances; next probe: spacing density, live update frequency
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer persistent sidebar or full-screen metric views; next probe: how to surface data without fixed nav taking space

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, focused atmosphere vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, gradual transitions vs. snappy, instant updates and alerts
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond better to friendly, encouraging voice (e.g., 'You crushed it!' vs 'Run complete')
facade.label: Conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: User values warmth and motivation in the app experience; design should feel like a supportive friend, not a fitness tracker robot
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers matter-of-fact, no-nonsense communication; design should be clean and functional without personality embellishment

facade.hypothesis: Runners need gesture-based interactions (hold-to-reveal, swipe-to-switch) over tap-tap navigation to stay in flow state during runs
facade.label: Tap and hold
facade.acceptImplies: Design gesture grammar: long-press for detail layers, swipe for metric switching, avoid nested menus that interrupt stride
facade.rejectImplies: Runners prefer explicit tap targets and immediate visual feedback; gesture-heavy interfaces feel risky at pace; prioritize large, direct hit targets

draft.title: RunWeather – Runner's Forecast Companion
draft.summary: A weather app designed specifically for runners, showing real-time conditions, air quality, and run-friendly forecasts with minimal distractions.
draft.html:
RunWeather

San Francisco, CA

72°
Condition Partly Cloudy
Humidity 58%
Wind 8 mph
Air Quality Good
Great for running Comfortable temperature and low wind. Ideal conditions for a morning run.

Next 6 Hours

72°
1 PM
☁

2 PM
☁
73°
3 PM
☀
71°
4 PM
☀
69°
5 PM
☁
67°
